section.title: The Service of the Levite Clans
section.sectionRationale: Building on chapter 3's introduction of the Levite clans, this chapter details exactly what each clan carries and how, especially the Kohathites' perilous handling of the most holy furniture, veiled first by the priests, completing the practical instructions for how Israel's mobile sanctuary actually moved.
section.thesis: God assigns each Levite clan specific, carefully bounded work, thirty to fifty years old, with the most holy objects always covered first by the priests before the Kohathites may carry them, showing that even faithful service has limits set by God's holiness, not human enthusiasm.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the Kohathites and the most holy furnishings, veiled by Aaron and his sons before they may be touched; the Gershonites and the tabernacle's curtains and coverings; the Merarites and the structural frames and posts; and the numbering of all three clans for active service.

units.label: The Kohathites and the covered furnishings

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses and Aaron,
“Take a census of the Kohathites among the Levites by their clans and families,
men from thirty to fifty years old, everyone who is qualified to {{service|serve}} in the work at the Tent of Meeting.
This service of the Kohathites at the Tent of Meeting regards the most holy things.
Whenever the camp sets out, Aaron and his sons are to go in, take down the veil of the curtain, and cover the ark of the Testimony with it.
They are to place over this a covering of fine leather, spread a solid blue cloth over it, and insert its poles.

Over the table of the Presence they are to spread a blue cloth and place the plates and cups on it, along with the bowls and pitchers for the drink offering. The regular bread offering is to remain on it.
And they shall spread a scarlet cloth over them, cover them with fine leather, and insert the poles.
They are to take a blue cloth and cover the lampstand used for light, together with its lamps, wick trimmers, and trays, as well as the jars of oil with which to supply it.
Then they shall wrap it and all its utensils inside a covering of fine leather and put it on the carrying frame.
Over the gold altar they are to spread a blue cloth, cover it with fine leather, and insert the poles.
They are to take all the utensils for serving in the sanctuary, place them in a blue cloth, cover them with fine leather, and put them on the carrying frame.
Then they shall remove the ashes from the bronze altar, spread a purple cloth over it,

and place on it all the vessels used to serve there: the firepans, meat forks, shovels, and sprinkling bowls, all the equipment of the altar. They are to spread over it a covering of fine leather and insert the poles.

When Aaron and his sons have finished covering the holy objects and all their equipment, as soon as the camp is ready to move, the Kohathites shall come and do the carrying. But they must not touch the holy objects, or they will die. These are the transportation duties of the Kohathites regarding the Tent of Meeting.

Eleazar son of Aaron the priest shall oversee the oil for the light, the fragrant incense, the daily grain offering, and the anointing oil. He has oversight of the entire tabernacle and everything in it, including the holy objects and their utensils.”
Then the LORD said to Moses and Aaron,
“Do not allow the Kohathite tribal clans to be cut off from among the Levites.
In order that they may live and not die when they come near the most holy things, do this for them: Aaron and his sons are to go in and assign each man his task and what he is to carry.
But the Kohathites are not to go in and look at the holy objects, even for a moment, or they will die.”
And the LORD said to Moses,
“Take a census of the Gershonites as well, by their families and clans,
from thirty to fifty years old, counting everyone who comes to serve in the work at the Tent of Meeting.
This is the service of the Gershonite clans regarding work and transport:
They are to carry the curtains of the tabernacle, the Tent of Meeting with the covering of fine leather over it, the curtains for the entrance to the Tent of Meeting,

the curtains of the courtyard, and the curtains for the entrance at the gate of the courtyard that surrounds the tabernacle and altar, along with their ropes and all the equipment for their service. The Gershonites will do all that needs to be done with these items.
All the service of the Gershonites, all their transport duties and other work, is to be done at the direction of Aaron and his sons; you are to assign to them all that they are responsible to carry.
This is the service of the Gershonite clans at the Tent of Meeting, and their duties shall be under the direction of Ithamar son of Aaron the priest.
As for the sons of Merari, you are to number them by their clans and families,
from thirty to fifty years old, counting everyone who comes to serve in the work of the Tent of Meeting.
This is the duty for all their service at the Tent of Meeting: to carry the frames of the tabernacle with its crossbars, posts, and bases,

and the posts of the surrounding courtyard with their bases, tent pegs, and ropes, including all their equipment and everything related to their use. You shall assign by name the items that they are responsible to carry.
This is the service of the Merarite clans according to all their work at the Tent of Meeting, under the direction of Ithamar son of Aaron the priest.”

units.label: All three clans numbered for service

verses.text: So Moses, Aaron, and the leaders of the congregation numbered the Kohathites by their clans and families,
everyone from thirty to fifty years old who came to serve in the work at the Tent of Meeting.
And those numbered by their clans totaled 2,750.
These were counted from the Kohathite clans, everyone who could serve at the Tent of Meeting. Moses and Aaron numbered them according to the command of the LORD through Moses.
Then the Gershonites were numbered by their clans and families,
everyone from thirty to fifty years old who came to serve in the work at the Tent of Meeting.
And those numbered by their clans and families totaled 2,630.
These were counted from the Gershonite clans who served at the Tent of Meeting, whom Moses and Aaron counted at the LORD's command.
And the Merarites were numbered by their clans and families,
everyone from thirty to fifty years old who came to serve in the work at the Tent of Meeting.
The men registered by their clans numbered 3,200.
These were counted from the Merarite clans, whom Moses and Aaron numbered at the LORD's command through Moses.
So Moses, Aaron, and the leaders of Israel numbered by their clans and families all the Levites
from thirty to fifty years old who came to do the work of serving and carrying the Tent of Meeting.
And the number of men was 8,580.
At the LORD's command they were numbered through Moses and each one was assigned his work and burden, as the LORD had commanded Moses.

service.gloss: Service, labor, or work, the same word used for worship and for servitude. Carrying tent poles and covered furniture is called abodah just as priestly ministry is, showing that physical, unglamorous labor for God's dwelling counted as true service to the LORD.

basic.context: This chapter details the specific carrying duties of the three Levite clans introduced in chapter 3: the Kohathites carry the most holy furnishings only after the priests have covered them, the Gershonites carry the tabernacle's curtains and coverings, and the Merarites carry its structural frames and posts.
basic.hermeneutics: This is practical instruction for how Israel's portable sanctuary was actually transported through the wilderness. The repeated warning that the Kohathites must never touch or even look at the uncovered holy objects, on pain of death, shows that access to God's presence, even for faithful servants, is strictly bounded by His holiness.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter repeatedly describes this work as abodah, service, a word that covers labor, worship, and even servitude, showing that carrying tent poles and covered furniture was regarded as genuine service to the LORD, not a lesser task beneath the priests' more visible ministry.
basic.typology: The priests covering the holy objects before the Kohathites may carry them pictures the necessity of mediation between sinful people and God's unveiled holiness, anticipating Christ, the great High Priest, who alone can bring people into God's presence without being consumed.
basic.christ: Where the priests had to veil the ark and the holy furnishings before the Kohathites could carry them safely, Christ tore the veil at His death, Matthew 27:51, opening a way into God's presence that this whole chapter's careful covering and distance could only anticipate, not provide.
basic.god: God is astonishingly detailed in His care for how His dwelling is transported, assigning exact ages, exact tasks, and exact safeguards for each clan. His holiness is protective as much as exclusive, His instructions here designed to keep even devoted servants alive as they serve Him.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 40:34-38 first describes the cloud and glory that make the tabernacle so dangerous to approach carelessly. Hebrews 9 describes the earthly sanctuary as a copy of heavenly realities. Matthew 27:51 records the temple veil torn at Christ's death, opening access this chapter's precautions foreshadow.
basic.oneStory: God provides careful, mediated access to His presence even for the Levites who serve Him most closely, requiring the priests to cover the holy things before anyone else may approach. That mediated access finds its fulfillment in Christ, whose priesthood tears the veil and brings God's people directly into His presence.

context.literary: This chapter is the practical extension of chapter 3, translating each clan's assigned duties into the specific objects each is responsible to carry, and it closes the whole priestly and Levitical unit that opened at the start of Numbers.
context.historical: The detailed carrying instructions reflect the real logistics of moving a large, valuable, and sacred structure repeatedly through desert terrain, requiring careful assignment of labor by age, strength, and clan across many years of travel.
context.canonical: The motif of the veiled ark and the danger of unauthorized contact recurs later in Israel's history, notably when Uzzah touches the ark and dies, 2 Samuel 6:6-7, showing this chapter's warnings were not merely theoretical.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assign practical, bounded responsibilities to each Levite clan for transporting the tabernacle, while underscoring through the Kohathites' example that even faithful service must respect the limits God sets around His holiness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical arrangement for a mobile desert sanctuary; it does not prescribe an ongoing pattern for how sacred objects must be handled today. Its abiding principle is that access to God's holy presence is always on His terms, never on human initiative or convenience.

debates.question: Why were the Kohathites forbidden even to look at the uncovered holy objects, on pain of death, verse 20?

views.label: A safeguard reflecting God's transcendent holiness
views.case: The instruction protects finite, sinful people from presuming familiarity with the very throne of God's presence, the ark and mercy seat, reinforcing the seriousness of unmediated contact with His glory.

views.label: A specific test of trust in the priestly mediation
views.case: Others note the instruction specifically trains the Kohathites to trust the priests' prior covering rather than their own judgment about what is safe to view or touch, a lesson in submission to appointed mediation.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the passage teaches dependence on God-appointed mediation rather than personal initiative when approaching what is holy, a pattern fulfilled fully in Christ's priesthood.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's repeated term abodah, service, applies equally to carrying tent frames and to priestly ministry, elevating the physical labor of transport to the level of true worship. The careful qualifying phrase so that they will not die surrounding the Kohathites' handling of holy things marks the entire unit with the gravity of divine holiness.

typology.type: The priests veiling the ark and holy furnishings before the Kohathites carry them
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the great High Priest, who alone mediates access to the Father, Hebrews 4:14-16, and who tears the veil at His death, Matthew 27:51.

typology.type: Careful, assigned service for each Levite clan
typology.fulfillment: The varied gifts and functions given to members of the church, each serving according to calling, 1 Corinthians 12:4-11.

study.christ: Where the priests had to cover the ark and the holy furnishings before the Kohathites could carry them without dying, Christ's death tore the temple veil, Matthew 27:51, accomplishing once for all what this chapter's elaborate precautions could only foreshadow: a way for God's people to approach His presence directly and live.
study.god: God's holiness in this chapter is protective, not merely exclusive: the covering of the ark and furnishings before the Kohathites approach is an act of mercy that allows faithful service to continue without death. His detailed instructions show a God who cares deeply about the safety of those who serve Him.

crossReferences.note: Describes the cloud and glory filling the tabernacle, the very presence this chapter's careful handling instructions protect against unauthorized approach.
Records Uzzah's death for touching the ark, showing this chapter's warnings were taken with deadly seriousness in Israel's later history.
Describes Christ as the great High Priest through whom believers may now approach God's throne with confidence.
Records the temple veil torn at Christ's death, opening the access this chapter's careful mediation could only anticipate.

study.oneStory: God provides careful, mediated access to His presence even for the Levites who serve Him most closely, requiring the priests to cover the holy things before anyone else may approach. That mediated access finds its fulfillment in Christ, whose priesthood tears the veil and brings God's people directly into His presence forever.

quiz.q: What had to happen before the Kohathites were allowed to carry the ark and other holy furnishings?

options.text: Aaron and his sons had to cover the objects completely first.
options.note: Verses 5 to 15 describe the priests covering each item before the Kohathites approach.

options.text: The Kohathites had to fast for a full day beforehand.
options.note: No fasting requirement is mentioned in the chapter.

options.text: Nothing; the Kohathites could handle the objects freely at any time.
options.note: This directly contradicts the explicit warning in verses 15 and 20.

options.text: The text gives no instructions about covering the furnishings.
options.note: Verses 5 to 15 give detailed covering instructions.

quiz.q: What was the age range for Levites entering active service at the Tent of Meeting in this chapter?

options.note: Verse 3 and its parallels for each clan state this range.
This does not match the range given in the chapter.
That range appears in a related but distinct instruction in Numbers 8:24-25.
The range is stated repeatedly throughout the chapter.

quiz.q: What warning is repeated concerning the Kohathites and the holy objects?

options.text: They must not touch or even look at the holy things, or they will die.
options.note: Verses 15 and 20 give this warning in almost identical language.

options.text: They must sing a psalm before approaching.
options.note: No such requirement appears in the text.

options.text: They must wash seven times before carrying anything.
options.note: No washing ritual is described in this chapter.

options.text: No warning is given regarding the Kohathites.
options.note: Verses 15 and 20 state the warning directly.

quiz.q: Which clan was responsible for carrying the tabernacle's curtains and coverings?

options.note: Verses 24 to 27 assign the curtains and coverings to the Gershonites.
The Merarites carried the frames, crossbars, and posts instead, verses 31 to 32.
The Kohathites carried the most holy furnishings instead, verses 4 to 15.

options.text: No clan is assigned this responsibility.
options.note: Verses 24 to 27 assign it explicitly to the Gershonites.

quiz.q: What was the total number of Levites counted as qualified for service in this chapter?

options.note: Verse 48 gives this combined total for all three clans.
That is the total Levite population a month old or older from chapter 3, not this chapter's service-age total.
That is the total fighting men of Israel from chapter 1, an entirely different count.
Verse 48 states the combined total explicitly.

questions: The Kohathites could only carry the ark once the priests had covered it; their access depended entirely on someone else's prior mediating work. Where in your life have you tried to approach God on your own terms rather than through the mediation Christ provides?

Every Levite clan had a specific, unglamorous physical task, carrying poles, curtains, or frames, yet it is called abodah, true service to the LORD. What ordinary or unnoticed work in your life could you begin to see as genuine worship?

The instructions here were severe: touching or even looking at the uncovered holy things meant death. How does the tearing of the temple veil at Christ's death change how you think about approaching God today?

Age and clan determined each Levite's specific responsibility; no one served outside their assigned task. How do you discern the specific responsibilities God has given you, rather than taking on tasks that are not yours to carry?

This whole elaborate system existed to protect people from a holiness they could not safely approach unaided. Where do you need a fresh sense of awe at how much it cost for you to have confident access to God?

translationNotes.note: They must not touch the holy objects, or they will die uses emphatic Hebrew phrasing, underscoring the severity of the warning rather than a mere possibility.
So that they may live and not die reveals that these detailed instructions are framed as mercy, protecting the Kohathites rather than merely restricting them.
The final tally, 8,580, counts only Levites currently qualified for active transport and service duties, distinct from the broader Levite census of 22,000 a month old or older in chapter 3.
